title: NATO Burden Sharing Enforcement Act
plain_summary: This bill would let the U.S. stop giving travel visas to people from NATO countries that don't spend enough money on their military. NATO countries are supposed to spend at least 2 percent of all their money on defense, but some don't do this. If a NATO country doesn't spend enough on defense, the U.S. could refuse to let people from that country get visas to visit America. This is meant to pressure those countries to spend more money on their military like they promised to do.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the NATO Burden Sharing Enforcement Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Discontinuing Granting Visas to Nationals of Country Failing to Meet NATO Obligations
sections.text: This section changes an existing immigration law. The changes allow the Secretary of State to stop giving travel permits called visas to people from NATO countries that do not spend enough money on their military. NATO is a group of countries that work together for defense. Each NATO country is supposed to spend at least two percent of all the money their country makes in a year on their military and defense. If a NATO country does not spend this amount, the United States can stop letting people from that country get visas to visit America. The section also changes which government official makes these decisions, switching from the Attorney General to the Secretary of Homeland Security in some cases, and giving the Secretary of State the power to make these visa decisions.
